25: The historical placement of Jonah son of Amittai from Gath-hepher as a prophet active during Jeroboam II’s reign — kîdbar YHWH ʾelōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer dibber bᵉyaḏ ʿaḇdô yônāh ben ʾămittay hannāḇîʾ ʾăšer miggat hāḥēper — confirms Jonah as a historical Israelite prophet in the mid-8th century BCE, a prophet to the northern kingdom before being sent to Nineveh. Jesus in Matthew 12:39-41 treats Jonah as a historical figure: kai kathōs ēn Iōnas en tē koilia tou kētous treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas, houtōs estai ho hyios tou anthrōpou en tē kardia tēs gēs treis hēmeras kai treis nyktas — ‘for just as Jonah was three days and three nights in the belly of the great fish, so will the Son of Man be three days and three nights in the heart of the earth.’ The 2 Kings 14:25 historical reference is the OT’s anchor for the historicity of the Jonah to whom Jesus appeals — the sign of Jonah (death and return from the depths) is the sign of Christ’s resurrection, grounded in the historical prophet whom the Kings narrative places in a datable reign.
1: Amaziah's accession in the second year of Jehoash of Israel — the synchronisms continue to weave the two kingdoms' histories together under YHWH's single governance. Each Judahite king is measured by the Davidic standard; none achieves it fully. The accession notices in Kings function as a running commentary on the gap between the promised Davidic king and the actual kings who fill the throne — a gap Christ finally closes.
2: Amaziah is twenty-five years old at accession and reigns twenty-nine years. His mother is Jehoaddan of Jerusalem — a Judahite queen mother, which gives him an undiluted Judahite lineage. The length of his reign is significant (twenty-nine years) but will be marked by a serious failure of pride (vv8–14). The contrast between long reign and moral failure is a recurring Judahite pattern.
3: "He did what was right in the sight of the LORD, yet not as David his father had done." The qualifying phrase — "yet not as David" — is the key. The Davidic standard is not merely a moral bar but a covenantal one: David's wholehearted trust (šālēm) is what Amaziah lacks. "In all respects he followed the example of his father Joash" — a double qualification: like Joash, not like David. The standard recedes toward perfection that no earthly king achieves; Christ alone is the king whose heart is entirely after the Father (John 8:29).
4: The high places remain — again. The persistent failure to achieve complete cultic centralization marks even the best Judahite kings. The people's continued sacrifice at local shrines represents the divided loyalty that Deuteronomy diagnoses and prohibits. The new covenant's answer is not a better centralized temple but the indwelling Spirit who makes each believer a temple (1 Corinthians 6:19), eliminating the need for any particular physical locus of worship (John 4:21–24).
5: Amaziah executes the servants who assassinated his father Joash — justice for treachery. The act is presented straightforwardly as royal accountability: those who killed the king are themselves killed. The rule of law within the covenant community is a shadow of the divine justice that YHWH will ultimately execute; no crime against his anointed goes unanswered permanently.
6: "But the sons of the murderers he did not put to death, in keeping with what is written in the Book of the Law of Moses: 'Parents shall not be put to death for their children, nor children for their parents; each person shall die for their own sin.'" Amaziah deliberately cites Deuteronomy 24:16 as the restraint on his vengeance. The principle of individual accountability — each person dies for their own sin — is Mosaic law at its most precise. The New Testament does not abandon this principle; it transforms it: Christ dies for others' sins voluntarily and as their substitute, not as a violation of the Deuteronomic rule but as its transcendence through willing self-offering (Isaiah 53:5–6; 2 Corinthians 5:21).
7: Amaziah kills ten thousand Edomites in the Valley of Salt and captures Sela (later Petra), renaming it Joktheel. The victory over Edom recalls David's subjugation of Edom (2 Samuel 8:14) — Amaziah is momentarily in Davidic territory militarily. But the victory inflates him dangerously; his next move is to challenge Jehoash of Israel (v8). Military success without wisdom becomes the occasion of pride and ruin. The pattern is the inverse of Christ, whose greatest victory — the cross — looks like defeat and produces no pride in the victor.
8: Amaziah's challenge to Jehoash — "Come, let us meet face to face" (nitrayʾeh pānîm) — is a call to battle. The verb rāʾāh in the hitpael carries the sense of a formal encounter that will decide status. Amaziah's Edomite victory has overextended his confidence. The challenge is a mistake that the narrative immediately frames as folly through Jehoash's parable response.
9: Jehoash's parable — "A thornbush in Lebanon sent word to the cedar of Lebanon: Give your daughter to my son in marriage. But a wild animal in Lebanon came by and trampled the thornbush." The parable is a miniature of wisdom literature: the thornbush presumes to negotiate with the cedar as an equal, and is destroyed by a casual passer-by. Amaziah is the thornbush; Jehoash is the cedar; Judah will be the trampled. The parable's wisdom is sharp: know your scale before issuing challenges. Christ never mistakes his scale — he empties himself, not because he overestimates others, but because his lowering is purposeful (Philippians 2:6–8).
10: "Yes, you have struck Edom, and it has gone to your head" (wᵉnāśāʾ libbᵉkā — "your heart has been lifted up"). Pride after limited success is the text's diagnosis. "Be satisfied with your glory and stay home." The advice is pragmatic and sound; Amaziah ignores it. The lifted heart (rûm lēḇāḇ) is a recurring warning in Deuteronomy (2 Kings 17:20) against kings who forget who gave them victory. Christ's heart is not lifted by success; he "did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped" (Philippians 2:6) even at his highest.
11: Amaziah refuses to listen. The narrative moves quickly from Jehoash's wise warning to battle, locating the confrontation at Beth-shemesh in Judah — already on Judean territory, which means Amaziah has either invaded Israel or Israel has pushed deep into Judah's heartland. The battle is on home soil, making the defeat that follows maximally humiliating.
12: "Judah was routed by Israel, and every man fled to his home." The complete rout — the covenant community self-destructing in internal warfare. "Every man fled to his home" is a phrase of total military collapse (see also 1 Kings 22:36); the army dissolves rather than retreats in order. The tragic spectacle of Israel fighting Judah, of YHWH's people at war with YHWH's people, is the nadir of covenant failure. Christ is the peace between the divided peoples — and in the eschatological vision, the twelve tribes are united again under the one shepherd (Ezekiel 37:22; John 10:16).
13: Jehoash captures Amaziah at Beth-shemesh and brings him to Jerusalem — then breaks down 400 cubits of Jerusalem's wall from the Ephraim Gate to the Corner Gate. The Davidic king captured, the holy city's defenses breached: this is a humiliation at the level of sovereignty. The wall that protects Jerusalem — the place YHWH chose for his name — is torn down by the Northern king. The city is exposed; the king is a prisoner. Only the New Jerusalem, whose walls are the glory of God (Revelation 21:23–25), will never be breached.
14: Jehoash takes all the gold and silver from the temple and palace, the hostages, and returns to Samaria. The pattern of temple treasury depletion recurs — Shishak (1 Kings 14:26), Jehoash himself (2 Kings 12:18), now Jehoash of Israel. The repeated despoiling of the temple mirrors the repeated failure of the covenant people. Christ's body, the new temple (John 2:21), cannot be permanently despoiled — the resurrection restores what crucifixion stripped away.
15: The regnal summary of Jehoash — "how he fought against Amaziah king of Judah" is one of the listed achievements — reflects the Northern narrator's perspective that this was a military victory. The irony is that the "winner" of this Israelite-Judahite battle contributed to the mutual weakening of both kingdoms before Assyria. Internal victories that leave everyone weaker are not victories at all; the prophetic tradition consistently identifies the true enemy as apostasy, not the neighboring covenant kingdom.
16: Jehoash buried in Samaria with the kings of Israel; Jeroboam II reigns in his place. The transition to Jeroboam II sets up the most prosperous (and spiritually stagnant) era of the Northern kingdom. Power and prosperity without covenant faithfulness is not blessing but deeper entanglement — a theme Amos will address directly to this generation. Jeroboam's prosperity is a gift of YHWH's compassion (v26–27), not a reward for obedience.
17: Amaziah survives Jehoash by fifteen years — a detail that complicates any simple theology of "obedience brings success, sin brings defeat." Amaziah was defeated, humiliated, had Jerusalem's wall torn down — yet he lives and reigns another decade and a half. The relationship between covenant faithfulness and earthly outcome is not mechanically predictable. Providence works through the complex pattern of history; its ultimate logic is disclosed only in Christ, whose suffering and death lead to resurrection glory (Luke 24:26).
18: The close formula — "the rest of the acts of Amaziah — are they not written in the Book of the Chronicles of the Kings of Judah?" — preserves the witness even of a king whose reign was marked by pride and defeat. The chronicle's fidelity to the record — including failures — is the narrative counterpart of the theological honesty that runs through Kings: the Davidic line produced many kings who fell short, and the text does not hide it, because the final David's glory does not depend on the interim kings' success.
19: Conspiracy in Jerusalem; Amaziah flees to Lachish; his pursuers kill him there. The same pattern as Joash his father (2 Kings 12:20): Judahite kings assassinated by servants/conspirators. Two generations of Judahite kings murdered from within — the covenant community devouring its own leadership. The vulnerability of earthly Davidic kings to betrayal from within is the shadow side of the messianic expectation: only the king who cannot be ultimately destroyed will secure his people permanently.
20: "They brought his body back on horseback, and he was buried in Jerusalem with his ancestors in the city of David." Even after assassination in Lachish, Amaziah is returned to Jerusalem for burial in the city of David. The covenant geography of the Davidic burial place is maintained even for defeated, murdered kings. The body is honored in death; the Davidic continuity is maintained in the burial ground. Christ is buried in a tomb and raised from it; his burial is real, his resurrection permanent — the fulfillment of the Davidic promise that "you will not abandon my soul to Sheol" (Psalms 16:10; Acts 2:27).
21: The people of Judah make Azariah (Uzziah) king at sixteen — a smooth succession despite the assassination of his father. The covenant community maintains the Davidic line even through crisis. Azariah will have a long and largely positive reign (2 Kings 15:1–7; 2 Chr 26); the dynasty's persistence despite repeated violence against its kings is itself a sign of YHWH's faithfulness to the covenant with David. No assassination can sever the line that will produce Christ.
22: Azariah "built up Elath and restored it to Judah" — a small territorial recovery at the southern tip of Judah on the Red Sea. The detail is noted as a positive achievement at the beginning of a long reign. The pattern of progressive territorial restoration and loss in Judah mirrors the trajectory of the covenant: periods of recovery followed by contraction, all within YHWH's governing purpose. The full territorial restoration awaits the messianic era (Ezekiel 47:13–23).
23: Jeroboam II's accession — "in the fifteenth year of Amaziah son of Joash, king of Judah" — places Israel's most powerful king within the synchronized history. Jeroboam II reigned forty-one years (the longest Northern dynasty reign), restoring the territory of Israel to its Solomonic extent. His power is remarkable; his theology is condemned in v24. The coexistence of maximum territorial reach and continued apostasy makes him the emblem of external success without covenant faithfulness.
24: "He did evil in the sight of the LORD; he did not turn away from all the sins of Jeroboam son of Nebat." The formula is unchanged despite the unprecedented prosperity of his reign. Power, territory, and prosperity do not alter the theological assessment — YHWH judges by covenant faithfulness, not by any other metric. This is the prophetic tradition's fundamental claim, which Amos and Hosea (both active in Jeroboam II's era) will develop at length: wealth is not blessing if the covenant is broken.
26: "For the LORD saw how bitterly Israel was suffering; there was no one left, slave or free, and no one to help Israel" (wᵉʾên ʿōzēr lᵉyiśrāʾēl). The divine compassion is awakened by the total helplessness of the people — not by their merit but by their extremity. "No one to help" is the language of abandonment (also Lamentations 1:7; Psalms 22:11) — the people utterly without a defender. YHWH's intervention in this situation is the pattern for the Incarnation: God enters the situation where no one else can help (Romans 5:6: "while we were still helpless, Christ died for the ungodly").
27: "But the LORD had not said he would blot out the name of Israel from under heaven." This is the theological ground of Jeroboam II's deliverance of Israel: not merit, not prayer, but YHWH's prior refusal to abandon his covenant name-promise. "So he delivered them by the hand of Jeroboam son of Joash" — a sinful, apostate king as the instrument of divine rescue. YHWH's faithfulness to his promise is not blocked by the unworthiness of his instruments. Christ himself enters through a genealogy that includes both saints and sinners (Matthew 1:1–17) because YHWH's faithfulness runs through, not around, human failure.
28: Jeroboam II "recovered Damascus and Hamath for Israel" — the Davidic-Solomonic territory at its northernmost extent. The prophet Jonah son of Amittai had prophesied this restoration (v25, though not listed in this verse). The fulfillment of prophetic words even through sinful instruments demonstrates that YHWH's word is not contingent on the holiness of its mediators. The word achieves its purpose (Isaiah 55:11) regardless of the instrument's character — a principle that reaches its fullest expression in the prophetic word becoming flesh in Christ.
29: Jeroboam rested with his ancestors; his son Zachariah reigned in his place. The closing of Jeroboam II's long reign is brief. The dynasty ends quickly after him — Zachariah is assassinated after six months (2 Kings 15:10), the last of the Jehu dynasty. The four-generation promise (2 Kings 10:30) is fulfilled exactly. YHWH's word holds even when the beneficiary of the promise is corrupt; his faithfulness to his word is not a reward for the dynasty's merit but a demonstration of his own character.
